questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: The function of a food technologist in a large marketing chain of food stores is to ensure that all foodstuffs which are offered for sale in the various retail outlets meet certain standard criteria for nonperishability, freshness, and fitness for human consumption. It is the technologist's job to visit the premises of suppliers and food producers (factory or farm), inspect the facilities and report thereon. His responsibility also includes receiving new products from local and foreign suppliers and performing exhaustive quality control testing on them. Finally, he should carry out surprise spot checks on goods in the marketing chain's own warehouses and stores. What conclusion can best be drawn from the preceding paragraph?

choices.a: A University degree in Food technology is a necessary and sufficient condition for becoming a food technologist.
choices.b: In practice, food technologist spends most time to check goods, must be rigorously tested.
choices.c: The food technologist stands between the unhygienic producer and the unsuspecting consumer.
choices.d: Home-produced foodstuffs are safer to eat than goods imported from abroad because they are subject to more regular and closer inspection procedures.
choices.e: Random checking of the quality of goods stored on the shelves in a foodstore is the best way of ensuring that foodstuffs of an inferior quality are not purchased by the general public.

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: There are three main factors that control the risks of becoming dependent on drugs. These factors are the type of drug, the personality of the individual, and the circumstances in which the drug is taken. Indeed, it could be said that the majority of the adult population have taken alcohol, yet few have become dependent on it. Also, many strong drugs that have been used for medical purposes have not caused the patient to become addicted. However, it can be demonstrated that people who have taken drugs for non-medical reasons become dependent on the drug. The dependence is not always physiological but may remain psychological, although the effects are still essentially the same. Those at greatest risk appear to be personalities that are psychopathic, immature, or otherwise unstable. Which of the following conclusions can be drawn from the text?

choices.a: Psychological dependence is very strong with heroin, morphine, cocaine, and amphetamines. Physiological dependence is great with heroin, amphetamine, less less with amphetamines, barbiturates, and alcohol.
choices.b: You cannot become addicted to certain drugs if one has a strong personality.
choices.c: Taking drugs for 'kicks' increases the possibility of becoming dependent on drugs.
choices.d: Psychological dependence is the greatest with heroin.
choices.e: Alcohol is a safe drug since very few people become dependent on it.

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: Sally overslept. Therefore, she did not eat breakfast. She realized that she was late for school, so she ran as fast as she could and did not see a hole in the ground which was in her path. She tripped and broke her ankle. She was then taken to the hospital and while lying in bed was visited by her friend, who wanted to know why she had got up so late. Which of the following conclusions can be made from the above passage?

choices.a: Because Sally did not eat her breakfast, she broke her ankle.
choices.b: Sally's friend visited her in the hospital because she wanted to know why she was late for school.
choices.c: Sally did not notice the hole because she overslept.
choices.d: Sally broke her ankle because she went to bed late the previous night.
choices.e: Sally's broken ankle meant she did not go to school that day.

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: The owners of a local supermarket have decided to make use of three no-redundant check-out counters. They believe that they will attract those customers who have lately been put off by the long check-out lines during the mid-morning and evening rush hours. The owners have concluded that in order to be successful, the increased revenue from existing and added counters will have to be more than the increase in maintenance costs for the added counters. The underlying goal of the owners can be summarised thus:

choices.a: To improve services to all customers
choices.b: To attract people who have never been to the store
choices.c: To make use of the redundant counters
choices.d: To keep maintenance costs on the added counters as low as possible.

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: Pioneers of the motor-car industry realised that if they were going to meet the growing demand for their product, they had to adapt the labour force used in the productive process. Instead of many men working to complete all the stages of one car at a time, they assigned defined tasks to each man which they would repeat on every car.

choices.a: Each worker became responsible for the same small task in the production of each car.
choices.b: More workers were employed in order to increase production.
choices.c: Workers had to be more skilled in order to make a complete car.
choices.d: The motor-car industry had to employ more men to make more cars.
choices.e: Many men work together to make one car and then do the same for the next car.
